text:
Language is at the heart of politics, from violent revolutions to bland press releases. Yet at the same time, nearly all politics is conveyed through texts. Many politics texts are also produced in enormous quantities, from the minutes of parliamentary hearings to the updates on the social media of politicians. Analyzing this content can be both extremely time-consuming and expensive, rendering many analyses that could be very valuable inapplicable. This chapter offers a primer of the (geeks of the) politics world on automatic content analysis and its approaches and pitfalls. While this technique was in the early days a specialized and infrequent tool in political science research, it is today—excluding exceptional cases that are the exception rather than the rule—impossible to imagine political science research as could be conducted meaningfully without it. All quantitative models of language have been proven to be wrong; they may very well be useful. They do not replace what we generally call “reading” a few important and relevant politics texts. There is no magic bullet method for analysing politics content; and at last—validity first.

This paper reviews a number of algorithms developed to analyze political language, among them dictionary-based approaches, supervised learning classification algorithms, and unsupervised algorithms for topic modeling and clustering. We will also describe some methods for scaling political actors to specific locations on the ideological space. To illustrate the potential and practicality of these approaches, we will give concrete examples of their application to analyses of Russian military language and U.S. Senate communications, and recommend several metrics for assessing the reliability of the resulting (automated) analyses. Our conclusion is that computer-assisted methods of the type developed in this project can provide cost-effective alternatives to traditional social scientific research methodologies. However, that the utility of these computational approaches is necessarily problem-dependent, and that social scientists are likely to need to develop additional computational tools for the analysis of political language in the future.
